posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Given the recent threads on wine prices and young folks getting into the appreciation of finer wines and more activity on this wonderful forum, here is my stab at building a quality, reasonably diverse cellar with reasonable wine pricing. While I realize $50 may be a lot for some, bear in mind that some of the wines on this list are what I call world class wines that cost a fraction of what some of their peers cost, and that can and will mature and age gracefully. I regularly buy every single wine on this list, and some in very large quantities and vintages. There are also a fair number of wines on this list that are around $25. Yes, my list is more Francophile, but that’s my palate.

Most of these wines should be easy to find. A few, like Lapierre Sans Soufre, take some looking, but that’s half the fun.

Bordeaux

Langoa Barton

D’Armailhac

Ferriere

Giscours

Cantemerle

Sociando Mallet

Lanessan

D’Issan

Lagrange

Cantenac Brown

Branaire Ducru

  • Vintages 2016, 2019 and 2020 are a treasure trove and widely available.

Chinon/Saumur

Baudry Les Grezeaux

Baudry Clos Guillot

Joguet Les Varennes du Grand Clos

Olga Raffault Les Picasses

Roche Nueve Franc de Pied

Breton Franc de Pied

Anything Huet for Loire whites

Beaujolais

Roilette Cuvee Tardive

Thivin Cote de Brouilly

Lapierre Sans Soufre

Bouland Delys Vieille Vignes

Southern Rhone

Domaine Charvin

Clos du Juncuas, Gigondas

Domaine du Cayron, Gigondas

Coudoulet de Beaucastel

Northern Rhone

Clape Vin des Amis

Xavier Gerard Le Blanchard, St Joseph

Champet, Cotie Rotie

Faury, Saint-Joseph (the Vieille Vignes bangs!)

Rousset Crozes Hermitage Les Picaudiere

California

Sky Vineyards Zin

Frog’s Leap Cabernet

Anything Edmund St. Johns

White Burgundy

Tribut

posts.name: Sam Kwak

Unfortunately JJ Prum may not be long for this list. I have not seen the 2023 Wehlener Sonnenuhr Kabinett offered for anything less than $60, although there are still plenty of prior vintages floating around various retailers. I would suggest grabbing any off the shelf you see while they’re still available at their more reasonable former prices. Of course they’ve long been underpriced compared to their quality but the price doubling in the span of 2-3 years is a little hard to stomach.

At least the good thing is there’s no lack - for now - of Prum’s compatriots that still bring fantastic value. Besides the German producers mentioned already, I would also add Max Ferd. Richter to this list.

posts.name: Justin Izzo
posts.cooked:
I’m enjoying thinking about this price category particularly as it might relate to people just getting started on their wine journey. Of course, there’s an ocean of stellar riesling available in this price range, but if you’re new to dry riesling then Emrich-Schönleber’s Mineral may be an interesting place to start.

I don’t think anyone has mentioned Sauternes yet. Many great producers can be had in .375 format in this price range (and sometimes even in 750). Lower demand has kept prices comparably low in this category, but these are always fun, shareable, quality wines. Perhaps it’s just my social circle, but I always get asked to bring Sauternes to dinner.

This is the part of the pool I swim in almost 100% of the time - I’ve only paid over $50/bottle for a handful of wines - so this thread is right up my alley.

I’ll echo all of the original Bordeaux, California, and Germany recs, the Baudry and Huet, Thivin, Produttori. Honestly I’d probably echo the whole list but I haven’t had all of them, so now I have more to seek out - thanks!

Some of the subsequent Cali recs are a little steep, despite being delicious - Ceritas, Littorai, Mayacamas, Rhys are all generally out of this range, though some are available on the secondary market.

Good to see Sauternes in the comments, too. Aside from d’Yquem, prestige cuvées, and some vintages of Rieussec/Suduiraut/de Fargues/Climens, pretty much everything can be found for under $50/375 mL. My top recs in this area would be Coutet and Lafaurie-Peyraguey.

Some other definite winners that I haven’t yet seen in this thread:

  • La Rioja Alta Viña Ardanza
  • Au Bon Climat Pinot Noirs
  • Ridge Zins - Geyserville being the obvious one
  • Meerlust Rubicon
  • Tablas Creek Esprit Blanc - pushing $50 now but still findable for less

For me, part of the appeal of our hobby, was finding good value wines that cellared well, and showed a progression. Some of those ‘values’ aren’t the value they once were, and I’d include in that Ch Musar, Produttori del Barbaresco (and many other langhe wines). Mastroberardino Taurasi Radici / Radici riserva .

If I were restarting the cellar today, I’d still include the following:

  • Soalheiro Vinho Verdes
  • Vespolina (especially from a tiny Ghemme producer called Miru)
  • Lamartine cahors (not the fancy, at times heavily oaked bottlings, but rather their pleasingly rustic standard bottling)
  • Timorasso from a selection of producers, though prices are edging up
  • Boasso (Gabbuti) Barbera d’Alba
  • Drei Dona Magnificat / Pruno Nero
  • Crociani Vino Nobile di Montepulciano
  • Ch Deyrem-Valentin
  • Foradori (the standard teroldego bottling)
  • San Patrignano Avi
  • Sidonio de Sousa Garrafeira
  • Gini Contrada Salvarenza Soave
  • Bleasdale Frank Potts
  • Te Mata Elston
  • a selection of sweet / dessert wines, a genre that seems undervalued for the pleasure they give, and the effort involved
  • Luis Pato Bairrada/Bagas. It may not be the amazing wine it once was, but the VV label in both red and white wine form is a very useful one in the cellar
  • Château de Minière Bourgueil
  • Hunter Semillon, even though it’s a pain to find
  • Macon whites be it Ch fuisse, Rijckaert/Rouve, thevenent or others. There’s value to be found here.
  • Tahbilk Marsanne
  • Wynns Black label Cabernet sauvignon
  • Rivera Castel del Monte Il Falcone
  • Xinomavro’s but not quite sure which would become that solid star.
  • Francois Pinon sparkling Vouvray (or frankly any Vouvray from that producer)
  • Selvapiana Bucerchiale Chianti rufina
  • Pewsey Vale contours riesling
  • Dom du Cayron Gigondas
  • Telmo rodriguez’s different ventures, perhaps more the ones outside of rioja
  • tiefenbrunner Feldmarschall
  • Angiolino Maule’s wines, albeit they can be a bit on the edge, so cellaring something of a gamble
  • Gaudio Grignolino
  • La Crotta or Les Cretes Fumin

although there are some aussies in there, their wines that I believe appeal to an old-world leaning palate.

That lot should get the cellar up and running without breaking the bank

Great thread! I’m new here and $50 is my usual drinking budget, so I’d add a few that haven’t been probably mentioned.

Red

  • Bruno Clair: Marsannay lieu dit might be at around $50 and it is one of the best red burg under $50 I’ve tried in recent years. Never tried their village Marsannay, but I suspect it’s not too different. I think this is the price range where Burgundy might be often a better QPR than American Pinot, depending on where you’re located.
  • Raul Perez in Bierzo makes a great wine out of Mencia. Ulteria Saint Jacques is one of best cheap wines I know (well below $30 or even $15)
  • K Vintners: the producer seems controversial but the wine is good. Not particularly super old world style, but their Syrah shows varietal characteristics very well and not too ripe. New oak is there but not too much to me. I think their entry cuvées don’t differ too much from their higher cuvées stylistically so some good options under $50.

Rose

  • Clos Cibonne: I don’t have too much experiences with rose, but this is one of the best rose I know and it happens to be way below $50. I have no idea why this is so cheap. All of their cuvees should be under $50.

If you are open to auction:

  • Bergstrom, Nicolas Jay, Hirsch and Ceritas for Pinot: All beautiful and balanced Pinots though their styles are different. Release price is $60+, but back vintage can be at $50 at winebid.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked:
Great idea for a thread. You could basically build an entire cellar of German wine in this price range. Yes Prum, Egon Müller and some of the GG’s are above this range but there is much more.

As Brian said I would highly recommend cellaring Keller Von der Fels. I buy multiple cases every year. It is around the $50 price. This is essentially GG fruit. Also if you can stretch a little the Keller RR bottling at $65-70 is a GG but because it just over the limit of RS to be a GG it sells for 1/3 of the GG.

One of my greatest wine collecting regrets is that I did not cellar massive amounts of Kabinett. Especially Kabinett Trocken from better vintages.

Willi Schaefer - When I ask winemakers in Germany what wines they buy. Every single one of them says Willi Schaefer. And you can still get their Kabinetts for well under $50

Ulli Stein - Buy everything from him under $50. A magnum of 2016 Blauschiefer was glorious last year.

Ludes - They have an incredible track record for aging

Martin Mullen - Excellent producer for both sweet and dry. In the U.S. you can only get them through Lyle Fass. If you are ok with having wines delivered twice a year (good for cellaring), I would highly recommend getting on Lyles list (Fass Selection) because he sells a ton of wines in this price range that are long-term aging wines.

I could keep going on an on forever with Germany…

Oh and if you can buy some of these in Magnums they will last 40+ years.

posts.name: Tomás Costa
posts.cooked:
I could write hundreds of posts on Portuguese wine alone in this category, but even if we take US availability to be a factor there are many excellent references. Apart from the obvious suggestion of Luís Pato, the following come to mind for around $50:

  • Quinta do Crasto Vinhas Velhas is probably the most widely produced Douro wine made from old vines I can think of, and it doesn’t compromise on the old vine character and complexity.
  • Rita Marques’ Conceito is another Douro favorite of mine, erring on the side of elegance.
  • Quinta das Bágeiras Garrafeira is the epitome of Baga: austere, highly structured, sort of “radically old world” wine that is within the aforementioned price range and will improve for years (2005 is currently singing).
  • Anselmo Mendes’ Parcela Única is the top Alvarinho from arguably the greatest of Alvarinho producers, and ages well too.
  • Nuno do Ó is one of my favorite winemakers of all time, and his Druida Encruzado Reserva is one of the most highly ranked Dão whites year out. Seems to retail in the US for $42-48.

Great thread! I’m new here and $50 is my usual drinking budget, so I’d add a few that haven’t been probably mentioned.

Red

Rose

  • Clos Cibonne: I don’t have too much experiences with rose, but this is one of the best rose I know and it happens to be way below $50. I have no idea why this is so cheap. All of their cuvees should be under $50.

If you are open to auction:

  • Bergstrom, Nicolas Jay, Hirsch and Ceritas for Pinot: All beautiful and balanced Pinots though their styles are different. Release price is $60+, but back vintage can be at $50 at winebid.

I mentioned Bruno Clair above, but only found very few Marsannay <50 on WSP.

Paying attention to this ratio is probably the best way to get a cellar that outpunches its cost. In Burgundy you might pay a 1,000% premium to jump from the village wine to the grand cru, so when you see the same jump elsewhere at like a 25% premium, it’s a no-brainer. Along similar lines, in Margaux, my favorites right now are Brane, Durfort, and d’Issan, all over the $50 cutoff but not by a lot. Accumulating Giscours and Cantenac Brown when the prices of the others haven’t caught up to their quality yet would be a big mistake.

posts.name: Terry H a r r i s
posts.cooked:
Cellar psychology should be factored in here. Speaking only from my own/personal experience, I started out “cellaring” under 12 bottles (wanting to have bottles available for a dinner, without instead having to head to the wine store after work that day, etc.) and slowly started accumulating more and more. But that was a very slow moving process - limited budget, spending priorities as young parents, etc. - and I suspect those same considerations exist for most younger folks starting out in this world of cellaring fine wine. Given all of this, I also think there is a certain bottle number threshold where it’s so much easier to keep one’s hands off that wine that needs or really benefits from 7 or 8 years of sleep, or 10 to 15 years of sleep, and so on. For me, I think I reached that threshold at about 200 or so bottles (but even then I managed to slaughter almost all of my Northern Rhodes way too young). Until that threshold is reached and maintained, I think targeting the category of “this bottle will be singing anytime within the first five years in my cellar” will be important.

Or something like that . . . .

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
An excellent thread - again - what a start to 2025!

Like @LasseK I’m never entirely sure how US prices compare with ours, but I’m reasonably optimistic that my suggestions will be in the $25-50 price bracket.

I’m going to stick to the only two French regions I know well enough, Bordeaux and the Loire. Robert and I share pretty well the same taste so I can happily confirm his entire list, with a few other ideas!

Bordeaux:

For Bordeaux novices, the best way to start is to try Crus Bourgeois or the equivalents on the Right Bank. These are relatively accessible young but can age beautifully, and often age as well, occasionally better, than classed growths costing a lot more (Robert’s Lanessan 82 is a prime example). The styles vary a lot, and you need to try them in order to decide what you like, but a $20 mistake hurts a lot less than a $50 one. My taste is very classical, and if you like higher ABV oaky stuff (which is not a criticism), look elsewhere - for example, Jeff Leve’s site will be a very useful guide. These are all CBs except where stated:

Caronne Sainte Gemme - sadly, it no longer exists, but older vintages are still easy to find.
Chasse-Spleen - not a CB anymore but worth trying, especially the 2016
Citran
Clément-Pichon
Fougas Maldoror - Côtes de Bourg
Du Glana - St.Julien - not a CB but an excellent way to try a St.Julien without paying a CC price
Larose-Trintaudon, or its stablemate Larose-Perganson
Liversan
Poujeaux - like Chasse-S, no longer a CB, also a Moulis, and capable of ageing beautifully
Rollan de By - the 05 tasted over Xmas was better than far too many CCs
Sénéjac
Clos du Jaugueyron - not a CB, and I would stick to the Haut-Médoc, at least to try, because the others are more than the price cap.

Loire whites

Muscadets:

Apart from Pépière, everyone’s favourite, I would also recommend:
Lieubeau
Bonnet-Huteau

Anjou/Savennières:
Ogéreau
Plaisance
Pierre Ménard

Vouvray:
Champalou

Loire reds:

Yannick Amirault
Domaine de la Chevalerie
Domaine de la Cotelleraie
Coulaine
Hureau
Frédéric Mabileau
Philippe Alliet
Villeneuve

Also, a lot of great producers make phenomenal, and sometimes expensive top cuvees, they also make excellent entry level wines that are still killer values and also perfectly likely to age into really beautiful wines.

I sometimes prefer these more because they are less polished (ie oak, ripeness etc.). Especially for drinking during the 5-10 year window.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Sarah_Kirschbaum:

Damn. This thread has made me buy a bunch of young Bordeaux, something I thought I was done with!

Since we know, you are not budgetary constrained(!!!), I highly recommend that you also look at the 2019 Durfort Vivens. B21 has this for $75, easily the best $100 and under Bordeaux made in 2019, in my humble opinion. I doubled down on this one after trying it, and then later I saw Keith Levenberg’s CellarTracker note, now I need to buy more. If his note alone doesn’t cause you to buy, nothing can move you!
